1 · SOC 2 — Scope, Control Catalog, Dashboard, Checklist & Roadmap

Scope — what's in scope, what's out, and why

▶

Scope decisions below assume a multi-tenant, cloud-hosted (AWS/Azure/GCP) application serving healthcare customers, operating as a HIPAA Business Associate, holding consumer accounts (PII) and clinical records (PHI/ePHI), with actual or planned EU users, and — where the software is placed on the EU market as a commercial product with digital elements — in scope for the EU Cyber Resilience Act.

1.0How to Decide Scope — Five Decisions in Order▶

Scope is the highest-leverage decision in the entire program: it determines audit cost, evidence burden, and what your customers can rely on. Work through these five decisions in order, and write the rationale down — the system description must defend every inclusion and exclusion.

Decision 1 — Type I or Type II, and how long a window?

Type I opines on design at a point in time; Type II on operating effectiveness over a period. Health customers almost universally require Type II. First report: run a 6-month window (minimum most auditors accept) starting only after core controls are demonstrably operating — a control adopted mid-window produces a qualified section. Steady state: 12-month windows with no coverage gaps between reports; a gap between report periods is a customer red flag.

Decision 2 — Which Trust Services Categories?

Security is mandatory. For everything else, apply the commitment test: include a category if (a) you make commitments about it in MSAs/BAAs/SLAs, or (b) a regulator makes the commitment for you. Availability: you sign SLAs and HIPAA §164.308(a)(7) mandates contingency planning — include. Confidentiality: PHI/PII handling is the product — include. Privacy: include if you have direct consumer relationships (accounts, DTC features); a pure BA with no consumer-facing surface can defensibly exclude it and rely on HIPAA, but document that reasoning. Processing Integrity: include only if the system computes/transforms/routes clinical values; storing and displaying verbatim does not require it. Each added category adds roughly 15–25% evidence burden — include what you can operate, not what looks impressive.

Decision 3 — System boundary: which systems, people, and processes?

Follow the data, not the org chart. Every component that creates, receives, maintains, or transmits ePHI is in (HIPAA's own trigger language), plus everything that controls those components: CI/CD, IdP, endpoints, logging, backups. The three classic under-scoping errors: analytics/warehouse pipelines fed with identifiable data, customer-support tooling where agents view PHI, and ML training sets. The classic over-scoping error: dragging the marketing website and PHI-free corporate IT into the audit — segment them technically, verify the segmentation, and exclude with documented rationale.

Decision 4 — Carve-out or inclusive method for subservice organizations?

Your cloud provider (and any critical subprocessor like a hosted database or email provider) is a subservice organization. Carve-out method (near-universal choice): their controls are excluded from your report; you list them, state the complementary subservice organization controls (CSOCs) you rely on, review their SOC 2 annually, and evidence that review. Inclusive method requires their auditor cooperation — impractical with hyperscalers. Also handle CUECs in reverse: your report will tell YOUR customers what they must do (manage their own user accounts, configure SSO); write those deliberately.

Decision 5 — One system or several?

If you run multiple products on shared infrastructure, one report covering the shared platform plus named products is cheaper and stronger than per-product reports. If a product has materially different infrastructure or data classes (e.g., one product holds PHI, another doesn't), separate system descriptions keep the PHI blast radius contained and the non-PHI product's audit lighter.

Scoping anti-patterns to avoid
  • Scoping to pass instead of scoping to the data: excluding the analytics pipeline because it's 'internal' while it holds identifiable PHI. Auditors trace data flows; regulators subpoena them.
  • Starting the Type II window before MFA, access reviews, or logging are actually operating — buying a guaranteed exception.
  • Treating the AWS BAA as covering every AWS service — it covers an enumerated eligible-services list; anything else touching PHI is an unauthorized disclosure.
  • Selecting all five categories in year one for optics, then failing Privacy criteria you had no program for. A clean 3-category report beats a qualified 5-category report every time.
  • Writing the system description after fieldwork starts. It's the contract for the whole exam — draft it first and let it drive the control set.
1.1Trust Services Category Selection▶
CategoryDecisionRationale
Security (Common Criteria CC1–CC9)MANDATORYSecurity is the only required Trust Services Category in every SOC 2 examination — it cannot be deselected. The nine Common Criteria carry the core HIPAA Security Rule overlap (access control, audit, integrity, transmission security) and nearly all GDPR Art. 32 and CRA Annex I technical requirements.
Availability (A1)SELECTClinical users depend on the application during care delivery; HIPAA's definition of security explicitly includes availability of ePHI, and the required Contingency Plan standard (§164.308(a)(7)) maps directly onto A1. Customer MSAs in health almost always carry uptime commitments — auditable commitments belong in the report.
Confidentiality (C1)SELECTPHI and PII are contractually and legally confidential. C1 evidences data classification, de-identification for non-production use, and provable disposal — the exact areas health customers probe in security questionnaires. Excluding it forces customers to take those on faith.
Privacy (P)SELECT (qualified)Recommended because the application collects consumer PII directly (accounts, device data, direct-to-consumer features), creating obligations outside the BAA channel: GDPR data-subject rights, FTC Health Breach Notification Rule, state privacy laws. If the app were purely a BA processing PHI on covered-entity instructions with no direct consumer relationship, many firms scope Privacy out and lean on HIPAA — document that decision either way.
Processing Integrity (PI1)CONDITIONALInclude when the application computes, transforms, or routes clinical values (results delivery, dosing support, HL7/FHIR interfacing) — wrong-patient or corrupted-value events are safety events. Exclude, with documented rationale, if the system only stores and displays data verbatim.
1.2System Boundary — Components In / Out / Carved Out▶
ComponentScopeWhy
Production cloud environment (VPCs, compute, data stores, KMS)INCreates, receives, maintains, transmits ePHI — the statutory HIPAA trigger; core of the SOC 2 system description.
CI/CD pipeline and source repositoriesINControls integrity of everything deployed to production (CC8.1); CRA secure-development evidence lives here.
Identity provider, MFA, endpoint fleet (MDM/EDR)INWorkforce access path to PHI; endpoints are where PHI leaves the cloud boundary.
Logging/SIEM and backup infrastructureIN§164.312(b) audit controls and §164.308(a)(7)(ii)(A) backup plan are required HIPAA specifications; backups contain full PHI copies.
Analytics / data warehouse / ML training pipelinesIN (often missed)If identifiable PHI flows in, every control follows it. De-identify at ingestion (C1.1) or accept full scope. The most common scoping error in health SaaS.
Customer support tooling (ticketing, session tools)IN (often missed)Support agents view PHI in tickets and screens; these SaaS tools need BAAs and access controls like any PHI system.
Corporate IT with no PHI path (marketing site, CRM without PHI)OUTExcludable if technically segmented and verified PHI-free; document the verification, and keep tracking pixels off any authenticated or PHI-adjacent surface (P6.1).
Cloud provider physical data centersCARVE-OUTSubservice organization under the carve-out method: physical/environmental controls are inherited via the CSP's SOC 2, reviewed annually (CC6.4); your BAA with the CSP is mandatory.
1.3Regulatory Posture of the Organization▶
HIPAA / HITECH
Business Associate

BAAs with covered-entity customers make the Security Rule directly enforceable against you and create breach-notification duties to each CE 'without unreasonable delay' (contractually often 24–72h). Required-vs-addressable specification decisions must be documented. Direct-to-consumer features that fall outside HIPAA are policed instead by the FTC Health Breach Notification Rule.

GDPR
Processor (for customer PHI) and Controller (for direct consumer accounts, employees, marketing)

Health data is Art. 9 special-category data: an explicit Art. 9(2) condition is required per purpose. Large-scale health processing triggers mandatory DPIA (Art. 35) and typically a DPO (Art. 37). Art. 28 DPAs with SCCs govern the processor role; controller duties (notices, DSARs, lawful bases) attach to the consumer-facing side.

EU Cyber Resilience Act
Manufacturer of a product with digital elements (if commercially placed on the EU market)

Main obligations apply from 11 December 2027 (vulnerability/incident reporting duties from September 2026): Annex I essential requirements (secure-by-default, encryption, attack-surface minimisation, secure deletion), a maintained SBOM, a coordinated vulnerability disclosure policy, free security updates across a defined support period, 24h/72h ENISA reporting for actively exploited vulnerabilities, CE marking via conformity assessment. Pure SaaS is generally outside CRA (NIS2 territory) — but shipped clients, mobile apps, agents, and on-prem connectors are squarely in it; healthcare-related products may classify as 'important', raising the conformity bar.

SOC 2 Type II
Service organization

Type II opines on operating effectiveness over a period (typically 12 months; 6 for a first report). Every control here must produce evidence continuously across that window — a control adopted mid-period yields a qualified section. Auditors sample terminations, access grants, changes, and incidents from the entire period.
